content: Just as linguistic reflexion (the core word meaning from high school English vocabulary learning) describes the double-layered process where light bounces back to form a visible reflection and thought turns inward to examine itself, LLM self-reflection mechanisms can be redesigned to explicitly leverage this linguistic-structural duality: first generating an output response that 'reflects' (mirrors) the user's prompt and task context, then turning that output inward as a new input to 'reflect on' the quality and logic of the original response. This dual-layered framing rooted in the word's inherent meaning resolves the common ambiguity in current LLM self-reflection design that mixes the two distinct functions of mirroring external context and critiquing internal output.
